preamble: The phonetic transformation from the voiced pharyngeal fricative /ʕ/ to the voiced palatal approximant /j/ represents a significant historical sound change, primarily attested within the Semitic language family. This shift involves both a weakening of articulation (lenition) and a substantial change in the place of articulation, moving from the pharynx to the palate.
phoneticEffects: Lenition (fricative to approximant), Fronting (pharyngeal to palatal), Palatalization, Debuccalization

examples.note: In the history of Akkadian, an East Semitic language, the Proto-Semitic voiced pharyngeal fricative *ʕ underwent a shift to the voiced palatal approximant /j/. This change is noted as having made the relationship between words and their roots less transparent. While specific word-for-word examples were not explicitly provided in the search results, the historical occurrence of this shift is attested.
